name: Trivy tag poisoning
description: An attacker force-pushed a malicious commit to a Trivy release tag, poisoning consumers that pinned only to the mutable tag instead of an immutable commit SHA.
detectionSignals: Workflow uses third-party actions by tag instead of commit SHA
Security-sensitive actions are not pinned to immutable digests
No policy enforcement for immutable action references

name: tj-actions/changed-files (Shai-Hulud) token exfiltration
description: A compromised GitHub Action release exfiltrated CI secrets through workflow logs, enabling unauthorized reuse of tokens and secrets in downstream environments.
detectionSignals: Workflows expose secrets to untrusted execution contexts
CI logs include sensitive values or token-like patterns

name: nx/Pwn Request
description: A pull request trigger abuse pattern allowed attacker-controlled code to execute in CI with elevated repository context, enabling tampering and secret access.
detectionSignals: Unsafe use of pull_request_target with checkout of untrusted code
Write-capable tokens available to PR-triggered workflows
No workflow isolation for fork-originated pull requests

name: Axios npm token leak
description: An npm publish token was exposed in CI logs, allowing an attacker to publish malicious package versions under a trusted package name.
detectionSignals: CI pipelines print environment variables or token-containing commands
Long-lived npm tokens used instead of scoped automation tokens
No log redaction or secret masking validation

name: Trivy force-push to main
description: An attacker force-pushed directly to the main branch where branch protection controls were insufficient, bypassing expected review and integrity checks.
detectionSignals: Default branch protection does not block force pushes
Required pull request reviews are disabled on the default branch
No status checks required before merge
